question: 6. Assuming that all the roots of the cubic equation \( x^{3}+a x^{2}+b x+c= \) 0 are real, show that the difference between the greatest and the least roots is not less than \( \left(a^{2}-3 b\right)^{12} \) or greater than \( 2\left(a^{2}-3 b\right)^{12 / 3^{12}} \).
solution:
Solution. Let the roots be ordered so that \( r_{1} \leq r_{2} \leq r_{3} \) and let \( r_{2}= \) \( r_{1}+u \) and \( r_{3}=r_{2}+v \) where \( u \) and \( v \) are non-negative. Then
\[
\begin{array}{l}
a=-\left(r_{1}+r_{2}+r_{3}\right) \\
b=r_{1} r_{2}+r_{2} r_{3}+r_{3} r_{1}
\end{array}
\]
and
\[
\begin{aligned}
a^{2}-3 b & =\frac{1}{2}\left[\left(r_{1}-r_{2}\right)^{2}+\left(r_{2}-r_{3}\right)^{2}+\left(r_{3}-r_{1}\right)^{2}\right] \\
& =\frac{1}{2}\left[u^{2}+v^{2}+(u+v)^{2}\right]=(u+v)^{2}-u v .
\end{aligned}
\]

Since \( u \) and \( u \) are non-negative, this gives
\[
a^{2}-3 b \leq(u+v)^{2}
\]

Since the difference between the greatest and the least roots is \( u+v \), this difference is at least \( \left(a^{2}-3 b\right)^{\prime 2} \).

On the other hand.
\[
u^{2}+v^{2}=\frac{1}{2}\left[(u+v)^{2}+(u-v)^{2}\right] \geq \frac{1}{2}(u+v)^{2},
\]
so
\[
a^{2}-3 b \geq \frac{3}{4}(u+v)^{2}
\]
and the difference \( u+v \) is at \( \operatorname{most}(2 / \sqrt{3})\left(a^{2}-3 b\right)^{1^{2}} \).
Remark. The proof shows that the given lower bound for \( u+v \) is attained precisely when two of the roots are equal, and the given upper bound precisely when \( u=v \), that is, when the roots are in arithmetic progression.

descriptive_long.question: 6. Assuming that all the roots of the cubic equation \( unknown^{3}+coeffa\,unknown^{2}+coeffb\,unknown+coeffc=0 \) are real, show that the difference between the greatest and the least roots is not less than \( \left(coeffa^{2}-3\,coeffb\right)^{12} \) or greater than \( 2\left(coeffa^{2}-3\,coeffb\right)^{12 / 3^{12}} \).
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. Let the roots be ordered so that \( rootone \leq roottwo \leq rootthree \) and let \( roottwo = rootone + gapone \) and \( rootthree = roottwo + gaptwo \) where \( gapone \) and \( gaptwo \) are non\-negative. Then
\[
\begin{array}{l}
coeffa = -\left(rootone + roottwo + rootthree\right) \\
coeffb = rootone \, roottwo + roottwo \, rootthree + rootthree \, rootone
\end{array}
\]
and
\[
\begin{aligned}
coeffa^{2} - 3\,coeffb & = \frac{1}{2}\left[ (rootone - roottwo)^{2} + (roottwo - rootthree)^{2} + (rootthree - rootone)^{2} \right] \\
& = \frac{1}{2}\left[gapone^{2} + gaptwo^{2} + (gapone + gaptwo)^{2}\right] = (gapone + gaptwo)^{2} - gapone\,gaptwo .
\end{aligned}
\]

Since \( gapone \) and \( gaptwo \) are non\-negative, this gives
\[
coeffa^{2} - 3\,coeffb \leq (gapone + gaptwo)^{2}
\]

Since the difference between the greatest and the least roots is \( gapone + gaptwo \), this difference is at least \( \left(coeffa^{2}-3\,coeffb\right)^{\prime 2} \).

On the other hand,
\[
gapone^{2} + gaptwo^{2} = \frac{1}{2}\left[(gapone + gaptwo)^{2} + (gapone - gaptwo)^{2}\right] \geq \frac{1}{2}(gapone + gaptwo)^{2},
\]
so
\[
coeffa^{2} - 3\,coeffb \geq \frac{3}{4}(gapone + gaptwo)^{2}
\]
and the difference \( gapone + gaptwo \) is at \( \operatorname{most}(2 / \sqrt{3})\left(coeffa^{2}-3\,coeffb\right)^{1^{2}} \).

Remark. The proof shows that the given lower bound for \( gapone + gaptwo \) is attained precisely when two of the roots are equal, and the given upper bound precisely when \( gapone = gaptwo \), that is, when the roots are in arithmetic progression.

descriptive_long_confusing.question: 6. Assuming that all the roots of the cubic equation \( pancakefl^{3}+evergreen pancakefl^{2}+sandpaper pancakefl+polaroids= \) 0 are real, show that the difference between the greatest and the least roots is not less than \( \left(evergreen^{2}-3 sandpaper\right)^{12} \) or greater than \( 2\left(evergreen^{2}-3 sandpaper\right)^{12 / 3^{12}} \).
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. Let the roots be ordered so that \( suitcasee \leq rainstorm \leq bookshelf \) and let \( rainstorm= \) \( suitcasee+lanternss \) and \( bookshelf=rainstorm+snowglobe \) where \( lanternss \) and \( snowglobe \) are non-negative. Then
\[
\begin{array}{l}
evergreen=-\left(suitcasee+rainstorm+bookshelf\right) \\
sandpaper=suitcasee rainstorm+rainstorm bookshelf+bookshelf suitcasee
\end{array}
\]
and
\[
\begin{aligned}
evergreen^{2}-3 sandpaper & =\frac{1}{2}\left[\left(suitcasee-rainstorm\right)^{2}+\left(rainstorm-bookshelf\right)^{2}+\left(bookshelf-suitcasee\right)^{2}\right] \\
& =\frac{1}{2}\left[lanternss^{2}+snowglobe^{2}+(lanternss+snowglobe)^{2}\right]=(lanternss+snowglobe)^{2}-lanternss snowglobe .
\end{aligned}
\]

Since \( lanternss \) and \( lanternss \) are non-negative, this gives
\[
evergreen^{2}-3 sandpaper \leq(lanternss+snowglobe)^{2}
\]

Since the difference between the greatest and the least roots is \( lanternss+snowglobe \), this difference is at least \( \left(evergreen^{2}-3 sandpaper\right)^{\prime 2} \).

On the other hand.
\[
lanternss^{2}+snowglobe^{2}=\frac{1}{2}\left[(lanternss+snowglobe)^{2}+(lanternss-snowglobe)^{2}\right] \geq \frac{1}{2}(lanternss+snowglobe)^{2},
\]
so
\[
evergreen^{2}-3 sandpaper \geq \frac{3}{4}(lanternss+snowglobe)^{2}
\]
and the difference \( lanternss+snowglobe \) is at \( \operatorname{most}(2 / \sqrt{3})\left(evergreen^{2}-3 sandpaper\right)^{1^{2}} \).
Remark. The proof shows that the given lower bound for \( lanternss+snowglobe \) is attained precisely when two of the roots are equal, and the given upper bound precisely when \( lanternss=snowglobe \), that is, when the roots are in arithmetic progression.

descriptive_long_misleading.question: 6. Assuming that all the roots of the cubic equation \( fixedpoint^{3}+variablea fixedpoint^{2}+variableb fixedpoint+variablec= \) 0 are real, show that the difference between the greatest and the least roots is not less than \( \left(variablea^{2}-3 variableb\right)^{12} \) or greater than \( 2\left(variablea^{2}-3 variableb\right)^{12 / 3^{12}} \).
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. Let the roots be ordered so that \( crownbot \leq crowntop \leq crownsky \) and let \( crowntop= crownbot+overlapq \) and \( crownsky=crowntop+mergeval \) where \( overlapq \) and \( mergeval \) are non-negative. Then
\[
\begin{array}{l}
variablea=-\left(crownbot+crowntop+crownsky\right) \\
variableb=crownbot crowntop+crowntop crownsky+crownsky crownbot
\end{array}
\]
and
\[
\begin{aligned}
variablea^{2}-3 variableb & =\frac{1}{2}\left[\left(crownbot-crowntop\right)^{2}+\left(crowntop-crownsky\right)^{2}+\left(crownsky-crownbot\right)^{2}\right] \\
& =\frac{1}{2}\left[overlapq^{2}+mergeval^{2}+(overlapq+mergeval)^{2}\right]=(overlapq+mergeval)^{2}-overlapq mergeval .
\end{aligned}
\]

Since \( overlapq \) and \( mergeval \) are non-negative, this gives
\[
variablea^{2}-3 variableb \leq(overlapq+mergeval)^{2}
\]

Since the difference between the greatest and the least roots is \( overlapq+mergeval \), this difference is at least \( \left(variablea^{2}-3 variableb\right)^{\prime 2} \).

On the other hand.
\[
overlapq^{2}+mergeval^{2}=\frac{1}{2}\left[(overlapq+mergeval)^{2}+(overlapq-mergeval)^{2}\right] \geq \frac{1}{2}(overlapq+mergeval)^{2},
\]
so
\[
variablea^{2}-3 variableb \geq \frac{3}{4}(overlapq+mergeval)^{2}
\]
and the difference \( overlapq+mergeval \) is at \( \operatorname{most}(2 / \sqrt{3})\left(variablea^{2}-3 variableb\right)^{1^{2}} \).
Remark. The proof shows that the given lower bound for \( overlapq+mergeval \) is attained precisely when two of the roots are equal, and the given upper bound precisely when \( overlapq=mergeval \), that is, when the roots are in arithmetic progression.

garbled_string.question: 6. Assuming that all the roots of the cubic equation \( qzxwvtnp^{3}+bxmaoqyz qzxwvtnp^{2}+lwdkprsj qzxwvtnp+uehqrzti= \) 0 are real, show that the difference between the greatest and the least roots is not less than \( \left(bxmaoqyz^{2}-3 lwdkprsj\right)^{12} \) or greater than \( 2\left(bxmaoqyz^{2}-3 lwdkprsj\right)^{12 / 3^{12}} \).
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. Let the roots be ordered so that \( hjgrksla \leq mvplaqre \leq tnswqkud \) and let \( mvplaqre= hjgrksla+vkzopmhe \) and \( tnswqkud=mvplaqre+yfrdscun \) where \( vkzopmhe \) and \( yfrdscun \) are non-negative. Then
\[
\begin{array}{l}
bxmaoqyz=-\left(hjgrksla+mvplaqre+tnswqkud\right) \\
lwdkprsj=hjgrksla mvplaqre+mvplaqre tnswqkud+tnswqkud hjgrksla
\end{array}
\]
and
\[
\begin{aligned}
bxmaoqyz^{2}-3 lwdkprsj & =\frac{1}{2}\left[\left(hjgrksla-mvplaqre\right)^{2}+\left(mvplaqre-tnswqkud\right)^{2}+\left(tnswqkud-hjgrksla\right)^{2}\right] \\
& =\frac{1}{2}\left[vkzopmhe^{2}+yfrdscun^{2}+(vkzopmhe+yfrdscun)^{2}\right]=(vkzopmhe+yfrdscun)^{2}-vkzopmhe yfrdscun .
\end{aligned}
\]

Since \( vkzopmhe \) and \( vkzopmhe \) are non-negative, this gives
\[
bxmaoqyz^{2}-3 lwdkprsj \leq(vkzopmhe+yfrdscun)^{2}
\]

Since the difference between the greatest and the least roots is \( vkzopmhe+yfrdscun \), this difference is at least \( \left(bxmaoqyz^{2}-3 lwdkprsj\right)^{\prime 2} \).

On the other hand.
\[
vkzopmhe^{2}+yfrdscun^{2}=\frac{1}{2}\left[(vkzopmhe+yfrdscun)^{2}+(vkzopmhe-yfrdscun)^{2}\right] \geq \frac{1}{2}(vkzopmhe+yfrdscun)^{2},
\]
so
\[
bxmaoqyz^{2}-3 lwdkprsj \geq \frac{3}{4}(vkzopmhe+yfrdscun)^{2}
\]
and the difference \( vkzopmhe+yfrdscun \) is at \( \operatorname{most}(2 / \sqrt{3})\left(bxmaoqyz^{2}-3 lwdkprsj\right)^{1^{2}} \).
Remark. The proof shows that the given lower bound for \( vkzopmhe+yfrdscun \) is attained precisely when two of the roots are equal, and the given upper bound precisely when \( vkzopmhe=yfrdscun \), that is, when the roots are in arithmetic progression.

kernel_variant.question:
Let  
 f(x)=x^3+px^2+qx+r (p,q,r\in \mathbb{R})  
be monic with three distinct real roots \zeta _1<\zeta _2<\zeta _3.  Set  
 \Delta :=\zeta _3-\zeta _1.  

(a)  Prove the classical two-sided estimate  
  \sqrt{p^2-3q} \leq  \Delta  \leq  (2/\sqrt{3})\sqrt{p^2-3q}.  

(b)  Show that equality on the left occurs iff f has a double root, and that equality on the right occurs iff the roots form an arithmetic progression.  

(c)  Prove density: for every \lambda  with 1<\lambda <(2/\sqrt{3}) there exists a cubic whose three real roots satisfy  
  \Delta =\lambda \sqrt{p^2-3q}.  

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
kernel_variant.solution:
Let \zeta _1\leq \zeta _2\leq \zeta _3 and put u:=\zeta _2-\zeta _1, v:=\zeta _3-\zeta _2 (non-negative).  Viete gives  

 D:=p^2-3q  
  =\frac{1}{2}[(\zeta _1-\zeta _2)^2+(\zeta _2-\zeta _3)^2+(\zeta _3-\zeta _1)^2]  
  =(u+v)^2-uv. (*)  

Since 0\leq uv\leq (u+v)^2/4 we obtain \frac{3}{4}(u+v)^2\leq D\leq (u+v)^2; taking square roots yields part (a).  

Extreme cases.  uv=0 (one gap vanishes) forces a repeated root and gives the lower bound; uv=(u+v)^2/4 (i.e. u=v) puts the roots in arithmetic progression and gives the upper bound.  No other configuration attains equality, proving (b).  

Density.  Fix \lambda \in (1,2/\sqrt{3}).  Choose u=t, v=(\lambda -1)t with t>0; then u+v=\lambda t and (u+v)/\sqrt{D}=\lambda  by (*).  Scaling x\mapsto cx and translating x\mapsto x+k change (p,q,r) continuously while keeping \lambda  unchanged, so suitable c,k produce a cubic with \Delta =\lambda \sqrt{p^2-3q}.  Because \lambda  can be any interior value, every intermediate gap actually occurs.  

This completes the argument and illustrates the sharpness and flexibility of the bounds.  

------------------------------------------------------------------------------

_replacement_note.reason: Original kernel variant was too easy compared to the original problem
